title: Forms
assignment: Review the concepts from this section. If something is unclear, go back to the lesson.
groups.0.title: The form
groups.0.items.0.def: <form> wraps the fields and makes them one. action says WHERE the data goes, method — HOW: post hides it, get puts it in the address. Rule: search — get, everything else — post. Whatever is outside the form is not sent.
groups.0.items.1.def: Every field needs a name — otherwise its data arrives nowhere while the form still says "thank you". name="" does the same. The two names must DIFFER — identical ones eat each other. Exactly the mistake that does not crash.
groups.0.items.2.def: type="email", type="tel", type="number" — the browser checks for you and the phone gives the right keyboard. required blocks an empty field. But this is CONVENIENCE, not protection: anyone removes required in two clicks. The server checks AGAIN, always.
groups.1.title: Label and choice
groups.1.items.0.def: A paragraph above a field is not a label — it just sits on top. <label> has for, the field has id, their value is ONE word. Click the label, the cursor jumps into the field. Without the link a blind person hears only "text field".
groups.1.items.1.def: <select> is a choice list, <option> is each choice. value is what gets sent; the text between the tags is what a person sees — the two often differ (sees "Mountain", sends "mtb"). If you know the answers in advance — select.
groups.2.title: Long text and button
groups.2.items.0.def: The big field for many lines. It has a closing tag, unlike <input> — and the starting text is BETWEEN the tags, not in value. So </textarea> is stuck right after: a blank line between the tags goes into the field.
groups.2.items.1.def: <button type="submit"> is real — Tab reaches it, Enter presses it, the focus shows. <div class="button"> is fake: without a mouse the form cannot be sent. Rule: leads somewhere — a link; does something — a button. If it needs code to behave like a button — it should have been a button.
